coredata.dc:title: Surface engineering of LiV3O8 with carbon quantum dots for enhanced electrochemical performance in sodium ion batteries
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Lingling
Niu, Yu
Zhu, Limin
Han, Qing
Qiu, Xuejing
Cao, Xiaoyu
coredata.dc:description: LiV3O8 (LVO), a prominent layered oxide that has been extensively studied in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), faces several challenges such as insufficient conductivity, irreversible phase transitions, structural collapse, and capacity degradation during charge-discharge cycles. These obstacles are further exacerbated in the context of sodium-ion batteries (SIBs), resulting in compromised cycle stability and rate performance, thereby hindering its application in SIBs. In this research, LVO/CQDs composites were efficiently prepared via a facile sonochemical method using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modification. The uniform dispersion of CQDs on the LVO surface, while preserving its bulk structure, enhances electronic conductivity and cycle stability, Coulombic efficiency, and rate capability through morphology and dimensional optimization. In particular, the LVO/10%CQDs cathode exhibits an initial discharge capacity of approximately 185.4 mAh g−1 at 30 mA g−1 and retains 116.5 mAh g−1 after 250 cycles, demonstrating remarkable cycling stability and rate capability. The integration of CQDs boosts the conductivity of LVO, reduces the internal resistance, increases the pseudo-capacitance contribution, enhances the Na+ diffusion coefficient, and significantly improves the electrochemical performance. Overall, this research presents a viable surface modification approach to enhance the electrochemical performance of layered metal oxides, potentially alleviating the challenges faced by LVO in SIBs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: LiV3O8
CQDs modification
Cathode materials
Sodium ion batteries
Electrochemical performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation of the synthesis of LVO/CQDs composites. (b) XRD patterns of LVO and LVO/x%CQDs (x = 5, 7, 10, 15, 20) composites. (c–e) High-resolution spectra of V 2p, C 1s, and O 1s for LVO/10%CQDs composite.
SEM images of (a) LVO and (b) LVO/10%CQDs. (c) SEM image and coorsponding EDX elemental mapping images for LVO/10%CQDs composite. (d, e) TEM images and (f, g) HRTEM images of LVO/10%CQDs composite.

(a) Cycle performance plots of LVOs and LVO/CQDs electrodes at 30 mA g−1. (b) Charge/discharge curves of LVO/10%CQDs at 30 mA g−1 over different cycle numbers. (c) Differential capacitance curves of LVO/10%CQDs at different cycles. (d) Rate performance plots of LVOs and LVO/10%CQDs. Corresponding (e) energy density and (f) power density of LVOs and LVO/10%CQDs.

(a) CV curves of LVO/10%CQDs for the first three cycles at a scan rate of 0.2 mV s−1. (b) CV curves of LVO/10%CQDs at different scan rates. (c) Linear relationship plots between v 1/2 and i p. (d) Linear relationship plots between log(i p) and log(v) at different scan rates. (e) Capacitive contribution of LVO/10%CQDs at 0.2 mV s−1. (f) Capacitive and diffusion controlled contributions of LVO/10%CQDs electrodes at different scan rates.

(a) EIS spectra of LVO and LVO/x%CQDs (x = 7, 10, 20). (b) Linear relationship between ω −1/2 and real part Z′ of LVO and LVO/x%CQDs (x = 7, 10, 20). (c) Time-voltage curve relationship in the GITT test of LVO/10%CQDs. (d) Sodium ion diffusion coefficient map of LVO/10%CQDs.

head.ce:title: Surface engineering of LiV3O8 with carbon quantum dots for enhanced electrochemical performance in sodium ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Engineering Technology Research Center of Electrochemical Energy Storage, Key Laboratory of High Specific Energy Materials for Electrochemical Power Sources of Zhengzhou City, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Engineering Technology Research Center of Electrochemical Energy Storage
Key Laboratory of High Specific Energy Materials for Electrochemical Power Sources of Zhengzhou City

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Engineering Technology Research Center of Electrochemical Energy Storage, Key Laboratory of High Specific Energy Materials for Electrochemical Power Sources of Zhengzhou City, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Environmental Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: LiV3O8 (LVO), a prominent layered oxide that has been extensively studied in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), faces several challenges such as insufficient conductivity, irreversible phase transitions, structural collapse, and capacity degradation during charge-discharge cycles. These obstacles are further exacerbated in the context of sodium-ion batteries (SIBs), resulting in compromised cycle stability and rate performance, thereby hindering its application in SIBs. In this research, LVO/CQDs composites were efficiently prepared via a facile sonochemical method using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modification. The uniform dispersion of CQDs on the LVO surface, while preserving its bulk structure, enhances electronic conductivity and cycle stability, Coulombic efficiency, and rate capability through morphology and dimensional optimization. In particular, the LVO/10%CQDs cathode exhibits an initial discharge capacity of approximately 185.4 mAh g−1 at 30 mA g−1 and retains 116.5 mAh g−1 after 250 cycles, demonstrating remarkable cycling stability and rate capability. The integration of CQDs boosts the conductivity of LVO, reduces the internal resistance, increases the pseudo-capacitance contribution, enhances the Na+ diffusion coefficient, and significantly improves the electrochemical performance. Overall, this research presents a viable surface modification approach to enhance the electrochemical performance of layered metal oxides, potentially alleviating the challenges faced by LVO in SIBs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the escalating consumption of fossil fuels and worsening environmental pollution, the widespread use of renewable clean energy has become increasingly crucial. Due to the intermittent nature of renewable energy sources such as wind, solar, and tidal power, rechargeable batteries based on alkali metal ion intercalation chemistry are widely recognized as an efficient method of energy storage. While lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) currently dominate the market for portable electronic devices and electric vehicles, the scarcity of lithium resources and its uneven distribution in the Earth's crust limit the widespread adoption of LIBs [1–6]. In response to this challenge, sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) have emerged as a significant alternative to LIBs due to their cost effectiveness, abundant sodium resources, and similar operating principles [7,8]. However, the significantly larger ionic radius of Na+ (1.02 Å) results in drastic volume expansion and sluggish reaction kinetics when incorporated into host materials, hindering the advancement of SIBs [9,10]. Therefore, the search for high-performance cathode materials for SIBs that can better accommodate the larger Na+ insertion/extraction volume is imperative.

Currently, the primary cathode materials in the field of high-power SIBs mainly include layered transition metal oxides, Prussian blue compounds, polyanionic compounds, and various organic compounds [11–16]. In particular, layered oxides stand out as a particularly promising choice due to their compact crystal structure, high specific capacity, elevated operating voltage, fast diffusion kinetics, customizable compositional properties, ease of preparation, and suitability for large-scale manufacturability [17,18]. In addition, their seamless integration into the prevailing LIBs infrastructure facilitates the rapid progress of commercialization efforts [19,20]. Within this field, LiV3O8 (LVO) emerges as a notable layered oxide that has attracted considerable attention in the domain of LIBs [21–24]. Studies have shown that the crystal structure of LiV3O8 consists of V3O8 units formed by the corner-sharing of VO6 octahedra and VO5 trigonal bipyramids arranged in a "sandwich" configuration with Li + ions sandwiched between two V3O8 layers [25]. Each unit cell accommodates two LVO molecules, providing two octahedral sites and six tetrahedral sites for Li+ insertion. Despite the inherent lithium deficiency, Li+ ions occupy the octahedral sites, enhancing structural robustness and maintaining charge equilibrium without leakage during cycling. During discharge, external Li+ ions populate the tetrahedral sites, facilitating reversible intercalation/deintercalation between the V3O8 layers, allowing each unit to host up to three Li+ ions and reach a capacity of up to 374 mAhg−1. However, the low conductivity of LVO leads to irreversible phase transitions, structural degradation, and capacity decay during charge/discharge cycles, limiting cycle life and rate capability and hindering further application [26–28]. In SIBs, LVO faces obstacles due to the larger dimensions of Na+ ions, which can hinder battery kinetics and affect rate capability, thereby limiting its exploration in the SIBs field [29,30]. Therefore, modifications to LVO are expected to improve its stability in sodium storage and enhance surface reaction kinetics, effectively addressing these issues.

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have demonstrated promising potential in the design of electrode materials for sodium-ion batteries due to their unique quantum size characteristics, abundant surface functional groups, and ease of surface functionalization [31–33]. Studies have indicated that carbon quantum dots can directly serve as the anode for SIBs, with their appropriate interlayer spacing providing insertion sites for Na+ and mitigating volume changes during insertion/extraction processes [34,35]. The small size of carbon quantum dots facilitates a reduced Na+ diffusion distance, thereby endowing the CQDs anode with a high sodium storage capacity. Moreover, the larger specific surface area and surface-enriched oxygen functional groups of CQDs contribute to sodium storage. Surface modification of carbon materials, metal oxides, and other electrode materials with CQDs can offer additional electrochemical active sites, enhance the conductivity of composite electrode materials, improve ion diffusion and charge transfer kinetics, and serve as sensitizers and protectors, preventing rapid aggregation of active materials [36–39]. Consequently, this enhances the structural stability of electrode materials, further boosting their electrochemical performance and opening up new possibilities for the design of high-capacity electrode materials.

In this study, a facile sonochemical approach was effectively employed for the synthesis of LVO/CQDs hybrids. The resulting one-dimensional (1D) nanorod structure provides a channel for rapid electron/ion transport. At the same time, the incorporation of CQDs leads to improvements in the structural properties, dimensions, and electronic conductivity of the LVO matrix, resulting in advances in cycling stability, Coulombic efficiency, and performance. When evaluated as a cathodic agent in SIBs, the LVO/10%CQDs composite delivered an initial discharge capacity of ∼185.4 mAh g−1 at 30 mA g−1, with a sustained capacity retention of ∼116.5 mAh g−1 over 250 cycles. In addition, the LVO/10%CQDs electrode exhibited exemplary durability over extended cycling conditions and exceptional performance at various discharge rates. As a result, the LVO/CQDs amalgam emerges as a promising candidate for superior cathode utilization in SIBs.

LVO was prepared by a traditional sol-gel method [29]. First, LiOH·H2O, NH4VO3, and citric acid were carefully weighed in a ratio of 1:3:4, thoroughly mixed and homogenized. The resulting mixture was then placed in a 50 mL vessel and its rheological properties were adjusted by the addition of deionized water. The vessel was heated in a hot air oven at 80 °C for 8 h and then dried in a vacuum oven at 100 °C for 12 h. The resulting substance was then calcined in a muffle furnace at 350 °C for 10 h at 5 °C min−1, resulting in brownish-red LiV3O8 (LVO). For the preparation of CQDs, 8 g NaOH and 40 mL acetone were magnetically stirred for 1 h and left at room temperature for 120 h, and then a certain amount of dilute HCl solution (1 M) was added to adjust the pH to neutral. After washing the product with ionized water for several times and evaporating at 100 °C for 12 h, CQDs powder was obtained.

LVO/CQDs composites were synthesized by the ultrasonic method by combining LVO with varying amounts of CQDs in mass ratios of 100:5, 100:7, 100:10, and 100:15 in a weighing bottle. Distilled water was added for initial mixing, followed by ultrasonication for 0.5 h and magnetic stirring for 8 h. The mixture was placed in a vacuum drying oven and dried at 100 °C for 12 h to produce LVO/x%CQDs (x = 5, 7, 10, 15, 20) composites.

The LVO/CQDs composites were extensively analyzed using a variety of methods. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku MiniFlex 600) was used to analyze the phase composition and purity. Specific surface area and pore size distribution were determined by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) techniques using an ASAP 2460 analyzer. Functional groups were identified by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Spectrum 400F), while surface composition and electronic structure data were obtained by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, EscaLab 250Xi). Micro-nano structures were investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Sigma 300) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos-F200S).

The mixture containing 80 % active material, 10 % Ketjen Black (KB), and 10 % polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) was ground evenly and placed in a glass bottle, then an appropriate amount of N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent was added and magnetically stirred for 12 h. The slurry was evenly coated on copper foil, vacuum dried at 60 °C for 12 h, then cut into 1.0 cm diameter discs with a mass load of ∼1.5 mg cm−2. The CR2016 button cells were put together using a microporous polypropylene membrane separator and a sodium foil counter electrode. The electrolyte comprised 1.0 M NaClO4 in a 1:1 volumetric ratio of ethylene carbonate (EC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC). Battery performance was assessed using a LAND-CT2001A multi-channel battery tester to determine specific charge/discharge capacities, rate capabilities, and cycle stability. Furthermore, kinetic studies, such as cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and galvanostatic intermittent titration (GITT), were carried out using a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology characterization of LVO/CQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis diagram of the LVO/CQDs composites is shown in Fig. 1 a. The crystal structure and phase composition of the LVO/x%CQDs (x = 5, 7, 10, 15, 20) composites were analyzed by powder XRD, as illustrated in Fig. 1b. The XRD patterns exhibit consistent characteristic peaks for all LVO/CQDs composites, aligning with the standard card (JCPDS: 72–1193) for LVO [40,41]. This observation indicates that the incorporation of CQDs does not alter the inherent structure of LVO. Furthermore, the XRD patterns of LVO/CQDs composites demonstrate a discernible trend where the peak intensity initially increases and then decreases with increasing loading ratios. In particular, LVO/10%CQDs manifests the highest peak intensity, implying increased crystallinity that could potentially augment the electrochemical sodium storage performance. XPS was employed to probe the elemental compositions and chemical states of the synthesized LVO and LVO/10%CQDs samples. Fig. S1a presents the XPS survey spectra of both LVO and LVO/10%CQDs, revealing distinctive peaks corresponding to V 2p, Li 1s, C 1s, and O 1s, confirming the presence of Li, V, O, and C elements in the samples [42]. In Fig. 1c, the V 2p peak in the XPS survey displays spin-orbit splitting into V 2p 3/2 and V 2p 1/2 components. The peak at ∼515.2/516.2 eV corresponds to V 2p 3/2, while the peak at ∼522.3/523.7 eV corresponds to V 2p 1/2, aligning with previous literature results [43]. Fig. 1d depicts the refined XPS spectrum of C 1s, which displays peaks at ∼283.4, 285.2, and 287.2 eV assigned to sp 2, C–O, and carbonyl (CO) carbon atoms, respectively, confirming the presence of CQDs in the LVO/10%CQDs composite. Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 1e) assigns the peak at ∼528.9 eV to the VO bond, while peaks at ∼531.6 and 539.8 eV are associated with O2, CO2, or H2O absorbed from the surrounding atmosphere [44]. In conclusion, the XPS analysis provides additional evidence for the successful fabrication of LVO/CQDs composites containing both CQDs and LVO components.

To examine the microstructure of the original LVO and LVO/x%CQDs composites (x = 7, 10, 15, 20), SEM analysis was performed. As presented in Fig. 2 a and b and S2, all samples are composed of irregularly stacked nanosheets or nanorods. Despite the noticeable aggregation observed in the pristine LVO, its basic morphology remains unaltered after the introduction of CQDs. As the loading ratio increases, subtle alterations in the morphology of the LVO/CQDs composites are discernible. In particular, the LVO/10%CQDs composite exhibits a more uniform structure with reduced fragmentation, suggesting a potentially more resilient configuration. Elemental mapping of the LVO/10%CQDs composite shows a uniform distribution of C, O, and V elements (Fig. 2c). The even distribution of carbon implies the successful integration of CQDs, which was validated by subsequent experiments. To get more insight into the morphology and microstructure of LVO/10%CQDs composite, TEM and HRTEM were performed. As demonstrated in Fig. 2d and e, the LVO/10%CQDs composite showcases a configuration of stacked ultrathin nanosheets with embedded CQDs, which appear as uniformly sized circular dots of about 5 nm in diameter [45]. Furthermore, the uniform dispersion of CQDs within the LVO/10%CQDs composite (Fig. 2f and S3), as shown in, signifies their successful integration onto the LVO substrate. This integration enhances the conductivity of the LVO electrode, promotes favorable interactions with the electrolyte, and potentially increases the Coulombic efficiency and rate capability. In addition, the HRTEM image in Fig. 2g reveals well-defined lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.642 nm aligned with the (100) crystal plane of LVO, providing conclusive evidence of the existence of CQDs and their successful integration.

The electrochemical performance of the LVO and LVO/CQDs electrodes was evaluated between 1.8 and 4.0 V with a current density of 30 mA g−1 using constant current charge-discharge experiments (Fig. 3 a). Initially, the specific capacity of pristine LVO was found to be 124.8 mAh g−1, dropping to 42.5 mAh g−1 after 250 cycles. Conversely, the initial discharge-specific capacities of LVO/7%CQDs, LVO/10%CQDs, LVO/15%CQDs, and LVO/20%CQDs are determined to be 151, 185.4, 148, and 140.3 mAh g−1, respectively. After 250 cycles, these capacities are 74.4, 116.5, 98.8, and 36 mAh g−1, respectively. These electrochemical results suggest that except for LVO/20%CQDs, the other LVO/CQDs electrodes exhibit improved electrochemical performance compared to pure LVO. In particular, LVO/10%CQDs displays superior cycling performance in terms of both initial discharge-specific capacity and capacity retention, outperforming the other electrode variants. Fig. 3b illustrates the charge-discharge profiles of the LVO/10%CQDs electrode for different cycles at 30 mA g−1 and a voltage window of 1.8–4.0 V. Compared to the LVO electrode, the charge-discharge voltage plateaus of the LVO/10%CQDs electrode remain predominantly unchanged, indicating that the introduction of CQDs does not alter the Na+ insertion/extraction mechanism within the LVO material [46]. Three pairs of charge-discharge voltage plateaus at 2.37/2.54, 2.82/3.15, and 3.34/3.50 V are discernible, corresponding to the Na+ insertion/extraction processes and related phase transition phenomena. Furthermore, an analysis of the differential capacity curves of the LVO/10%CQDs electrode at different cycle numbers reveals the presence of three pairs of oxidation-reduction peaks, consistent with the charge-discharge profiles. The area and position of these peaks show minimal variation throughout the charge-discharge process, indicating robust oxidation-reduction reversibility and structural integrity of the LVO/10%CQDs electrode (Fig. 3d).

The LVO/10%CQDs electrode exhibits improved rate capability as depicted in Fig. 3c. Specifically, at current densities of 30, 60, 120, 240, 480, and 960 mA g−1, the LVO/10%CQDs electrode showcases higher discharge-specific capacities compared to LVO, with average values of 152.36, 122.4, 102.96, 85.87, 72.53, and 64.36 mAh g−1, respectively. Although LVO/10%CQDs have a lower capacity retention rate after 70 cycles (62.4 %) than LVO (69.4 %), the discharge-specific capacity of the LVO electrode recovers to 76.69 mAh g−1 upon return to 30 mA g−1, whereas that of the LVO/10%CQDs electrode recovers to 108.09 mAh g−1, underscoring the superior electrochemical reversibility of LVO/10%CQDs over LVO. Additionally, the energy density and power density of LVO/10%CQDs (222.6–477.4 Wh kg−1 and 130.1–1060.0 W kg−1) are significantly higher than that of LVO (162.4–309.8 Wh kg−1 and 90.3–955.3 W kg−1) based on the cathode side at different current densities, as shown in Fig. 3e and f, indicating that LVO/10%CQDS has outstanding practical application potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical kinetics analysis of LVO/CQDs cathodes
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical kinetics properties of the LVO/10%CQDs electrode were investigated by CV over a voltage range from 1.8 to 4.0 V (vs. Na/Na+) with a scan rate of 0.2 mV s−1 (Fig. 4 a). Evaluation of the CV curves for the initial three cycles of the LVO/10%CQDs electrode reveals almost complete overlap, signifying superb redox reversibility and durability. In particular, the CV curves show three distinct sets of redox peaks positioned at ∼2.40/2.56, 2.86/3.13, and 3.37/3.46 V, corresponding to the repeated Na+ intercalation-deintercalation processes, consistent with the charge-discharge voltage plateaus [29]. The CV curves of LVO/10%CQDs at different scan rates display peak currents that are positively correlated with the scan rate, while the peak shapes remain largely unchanged (Fig. 4b). Nevertheless, with escalating scan rates, the reduction peaks exhibit a marginal shift towards lower potentials, and the oxidation peaks shift slightly towards higher potentials, indicating a small polarization influence. This finding underscores the exceptional electrochemical robustness and reversibility of the LVO/10%CQDs electrode. Fig. 4c illustrates a linear correlation between v 1/2 and peak current i p of the LVO/10%CQDs electrode, indicating a strong linear relationship between these two parameters. This correlation implies a close relationship between the redox process and ion diffusion.

The impressive ability of LVO/10%CQDs to sustain high power rates is due to the capacitive effect, which can be quantified using Eq (1). Where i p represents the maximum electrical current, v denotes the scan velocity, and b is determined from the linear correlation between log(i) and log(v). Typically, a b value of 0.5 denotes a diffusion-limited electrochemical reaction mechanism, while a b value of 1.0 signifies a process primarily governed by pseudocapacitance [47]. The correlation between log(i) and log(v) is illustrated in Fig. 4d, with b values of 0.64 and 0.66 for the oxidation and reduction processes, respectively. These values emphasize the presence of pseudocapacitive behavior in the LVO/10%CQDs electrode. To evaluate the effect of the pseudocapacitance phenomenon on the total capacitance, calculations can be made based on Eqs (2) and (3). For LVO/10%CQDs, the pseudo-capacitance contribution at 0.2 mV s−1 is approximately 56.6 %, as illustrated in Fig. 4e. Fig. 4f plots the pseudo-capacitance contribution of LVO/10%CQDs over different scan rates, with percentages of 61.2 %, 62.6 %, 62.9 %, and 63.6 % at scan rates of 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1.0 mV s−1, respectively. The pattern observed indicates an escalation of the pseudo-capacitance contribution with increasing scan rate. This trend is attributed to the reduced duration of Na+ insertion/extraction cycles at higher scan rates, leading to a lack of sufficient time for all active sites to engage in the reaction, thereby amplifying the pseudocapacitive behavior.
(1)
i p = a v b
(2)
i = k 1 v + k 2 v 1/2
(3)
i/ v 1/ 2 = k 1 v 1/2 + k 2

EIS measurements were performed to gain deeper insight into the ion diffusion and charge transfer processes. Fig. 5 a shows EIS plots and equivalent circuit diagrams for both LVO and LVO/x%CQDs (x = 7, 10, 20) composites under open circuit voltage conditions. The EIS plots display a linear slope at lower frequencies and a semicircle at higher frequencies. The peak of the impedance plot represents the resistance of the electrolyte in the battery system (R s). The R SEI and CPE1 components that form the high-frequency semicircle are associated with ion diffusion and migration within the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) film [48]. The electrode interface's charge transfer process is represented by R ct and CPE2, marked by the endpoint of the semicircle in the impedance plot. W s represents the Warburg impedance related to the diffusion kinetics of Na+ in the solid phase [49]. The linear trend observed in the low-frequency range reveals the Na+ diffusion behavior within the materials. A comparative analysis of the fitted data shows that the R ct values of LVO/CQDs composites (7%: 1194 Ω, 10%: 412 Ω, 20%: 1950 Ω) are markedly lower than those of pure LVO (2720 Ω), implying that the incorporation of CQDs reduces the charge transfer resistance of LVO, thereby enhancing the reaction kinetics [50]. In particular, the LVO/10%CQDs electrode exhibits the lowest R ct value, which underscores its superior electrochemical performance. The relationship between Z′ and the above resistance can be expressed mathematically by Eq (4). Fig. 5b shows the linear relationship between ω −1/2 and the real part of Z′ for LVO and LVO/x%CQDs (x = 7, 10, 20), along with the corresponding fitted curves. The slope of the fitted curve, denoted as σ in eq (4), is minimum for LVO/10%CQDs. According to Eq (5), the Na+ diffusion coefficient (D Na+) has an inverse relationship with σ, implying that the D Na+ is higher for LVO/10%CQDs, which is beneficial for the electrochemical performance of the material.
(4)
Z' = R e + R f + R ct +σω −1/2
(5)
D = 0.5R 2 T 2 /S 2 n 4 F 4 c 2 σ 2
(6)
D =
4
π τ
(
m B
V M
M B
S
)
2
(
Δ
E S
Δ
E t
)
2

Furthermore, the diffusion coefficient of Na+ in LVO/10%CQDs cathode was determined by GITT measurements. Fig. 5c shows the voltage-time curve during the fourth charge-discharge cycle of LVO/10%CQDs and LVO. Here, ΔE s represents the voltage change induced by the pulse [51], iR drop is the voltage discrepancy between the charge or discharge state and the resting voltage, and ΔE t reflects the voltage difference during the charge or discharge process. The diffusion coefficient of Na+ (D Na+) can be determined from Eq (6). In this equation, the molar weight, volume per mole, and duration of steady electric flow for LVO/10%CQDs are denoted as m B, V M, and τ, respectively, with S indicating the active surface area. The values of ΔE s and ΔE t can be calculated from the GITT curve. The calculation results show that the D Na+ of LVO/10%CQDs is on the order of 10−11 cm2 s−1, while the D Na+ of the pure LVO is on the order of 10−12 cm2 s−1 (Fig. 5d and S4). Notably, the D Na+ of LVO/10%CQDs is significantly higher than that of LVO, demonstrating that the composite CQDs effectively boost the D Na+ of LVO, which is consistent with the electrochemical performance results.

In brief, a series of LVO/CQDs composites were synthesized by a simple sonochemical method and comprehensively characterized to verify the uniform distribution of CQDs on the LVO surface without compromising the structural stability of the original material. The optimal LVO/10%CQDs composite, used as a cathode in SIBs, exhibited an impressive initial discharge specific capacity of 185.4 mAh g−1 with a capacity retention rate of 63% over 250 cycles. In particular, the LVO/10%CQDs electrode showed superb long-term performance and capacity to handle varying discharge rates. Subsequent analysis revealed that the integration of CQDs enhanced the electrical conductivity of LVO, reduced the internal resistance, increased the pseudocapacitive contributions, and accelerated a superior Na+ diffusion coefficient. These enhancements significantly boosted the electrochemical performance of the LVO/CQD composites. This innovative approach holds significant potential for the application of CQD-modified composites in cathode materials, thereby expanding the range of cathode options in energy storage implementations.

Lingling Xie: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yu Niu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Limin Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Qing Han: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Xuejing Qiu: Investigation. Xiaoyu Cao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation, China (Nos. 52071132, U21A20284 and 52261135632), Natural Science Foundation of Henan, China (Nos. 232300421080, 242300421035), Program for Innovative Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Henan Province, China (No. 24IRTSTHN006), Key Scientific Research Programs in Universities of Henan Province, China–Special Projects for Basic Research (No. 23ZX008), and the Ph.D. Programs Foundation of Henan University of Technology, China (No. 2021BS0027).
